figureTitle: Bastani three-arm outcome
figureType: statistical (grouped/paired bar chart)
desc: Identical AI technology produces opposite learning outcomes depending on whether it is guardrailed: practice-period gain versus unassisted-exam change across three experimental arms.
subtitle: Practice change vs. unassisted-exam change, signed against zero

figureTitle: Two rulers for the same effect size
figureType: comparison panels (two distribution panels)
desc: The same d=0.40 reads as impressive or exceptional depending on whether it sits against Hattie's inflated synthesis distribution or Kraft's realistic field-experiment distribution.
subtitle: One fixed d=0.40 marker on two distributions

rows.right: d=0.40 lands far in the high tail

figureTitle: Four AI-feasibility classes across the list
figureType: statistical (paired stacked bars)
desc: The 252 influences distribute unevenly across four AI-feasibility classes, and the high-impact subset is dominated by AUGMENTED rather than NATIVE — augmentation territory, not replacement territory.
subtitle: All 252 influences vs. the above-hinge subset (122)

figureTitle: Anatomy of one framework row
figureType: annotated example (single row schematic)
desc: A single influence row carries seven columns of three epistemic kinds — measured, classified, and hypothesized — and the hypothesized cell must be visually unmistakable from the measured one.
subtitle: Seven cells, three epistemic kinds

figureTitle: Three bands inside one drawer
figureType: comparison panels (three stacked range bands)
desc: The 130 below-hinge influences are three structurally different kinds — modest-but-real, negligible, and harmful — distinguished by effect-size band and by whether each entry is a choice or a condition.
subtitle: 130 below-hinge influences split by effect-size band

layers.label: Harmful band
layers.note: 17 influences, d -0.01 to -0.90; conditions vs. policy choices

figureTitle: Four routes to below-hinge
figureType: conceptual map (four-route decision fan)
desc: Every below-hinge number arrived by one of four distinct routes, each demanding an opposite response, so a single influence must be sorted by which route applies before any judgment.
subtitle: One entry node fans to four route-to-response pairs

figureTitle: The fading worked-example sequence
figureType: flowchart (three-stage horizontal progression)
desc: Worked examples should fade across three stages — full solution, completion problem, unsupported problem — with the expertise-reversal boundary marking where scaffolding must be retired.
subtitle: Scaffolding shrinks; expertise-reversal divides stages 2 and 3

data.note: Scaffolding region shrinks across the sequence

figureTitle: Phone-ban effect by subgroup
figureType: statistical (bar chart by subgroup)
desc: A mobile-phone-ban's average effect conceals strong heterogeneity — concentrated in low achievers, near-zero for high achievers — which the single mid-pack number hides entirely.
subtitle: Beland & Murphy (2016), exam-gain effect size

figureTitle: The spacing effect crossover
figureType: statistical (two-line retention chart)
desc: Spaced practice produces lower immediate recall but dramatically higher delayed recall than massed practice — the two retention curves cross, and the strain is the mechanism.
subtitle: Recall accuracy over days after study

figureTitle: Division of labor: logistics vs. retrieval
figureType: systems diagram (perimeter-and-core)
desc: AI runs the separable logistics around a practice routine — scheduling, varying, recording, flagging — while the learner's retrieval moment and the teacher's selection judgment stay structurally protected.
subtitle: AI arrows stop at the boundary; teacher judgment crosses it

figureTitle: Reclaimed-hours accounting
figureType: statistical (before/after stacked bar)
desc: The tool's value appears only when planning-period time is accounted for — hours shift away from calendar-building and item-writing toward student-work analysis and feedback.
subtitle: Planning-period time before vs. after an automated review tool

figureTitle: Who does the transforming?
figureType: flowchart (two-path causal fork)
desc: When the student builds structure first and AI critiques it, transfer appears on a cold assessment; when AI generates structure and the student fills it in, transfer does not — the fork is who performs the transforming act.
subtitle: Two paths from one transforming decision

stages.label: Student builds structure / AI generates structure
AI evaluates, student revises / student fills in
Transfer appears vs. transfer blocked on cold assessment

data.note: Path A builds transfer; Path B blocks it

figureTitle: The self-regulation control loop
figureType: cycle (closed control loop)
desc: Metacognition is a closed control cycle — set goal, attempt, monitor, adjust, evaluate — and the learner must run every segment, or an external regulator runs the loop while the learner's capacity atrophies.
subtitle: The learner powers every segment

figureTitle: The fading schedule across four essays
figureType: timeline (declining-scaffold progression)
desc: Scaffold intensity must decline across successive tasks — full scaffold, reduced, student-self-critique-first, AI-as-check-only — so the prompts thin out as the learner demonstrates unprompted self-monitoring.
subtitle: AI scaffold contracts as learner-led work expands

markers.label: AI used only to check the student's critique

figureTitle: Description vs. recipe
figureType: comparison panels (two-column contrast)
desc: The same d=1.33 warrants opposite readings — a correlational description of a student asset (judgment accuracy) versus a vendor's causal claim of a purchasable payoff.
subtitle: One d=1.33 value, two structurally different readings

rows.left: Judgment accuracy as a pre-existing asset

figureTitle: The calibration flywheel
figureType: cycle (flywheel loop with error node)
desc: A self-regulated learner runs a loop — predict, plan, attempt, monitor, evaluate, adjust — where each cycle improves the work and sharpens the model of self, and the prediction-error is the loop's most valuable output.
subtitle: Prediction-error is the curriculum

figureTitle: Safe role vs. forbidden substitution
figureType: structural schematic (paired-column ladder)
desc: At each loop segment AI has a legitimate logistics-and-feedback role and a forbidden first-person substitution, and the gate runs cleanly between them.
subtitle: Safe AI role (left) vs. forbidden substitution (right), per segment

rows.right: Make the prediction for the learner

figureTitle: Detection pipeline vs. response pipeline
figureType: systems diagram (two-pipeline flow with single handoff)
desc: AI completes the detection pipeline entirely (context variables, model, risk score, flag) but touches nothing in the response pipeline; the two systems connect only at human review.
subtitle: Single handoff: flag to human review

figureTitle: Working-memory load reduction in a task
figureType: annotated example (before/after structural schematic)
desc: The group's one genuine lever is task redesign — the same multi-step procedure made to fit a narrower working-memory channel by externalizing steps and adding an adjacent worked example.
subtitle: Before: four steps held in mind. After: steps externalized

rows.left: Four steps held simultaneously in mind
rows.right: Steps externalized as an ordered list
Adjacent worked example beside the problem

figureTitle: The asymmetry of trust
figureType: statistical (two-timeline trajectory chart)
desc: Authentic relational investment compounds slowly into high trust, while a simulated relationship's metrics rise then collapse below the starting baseline in a single discovery event.
subtitle: Slow compounding vs. instantaneous collapse below baseline

figureTitle: AI on the perimeter, human at the event
figureType: systems diagram (before/after perimeter around a protected core)
desc: AI's legitimate role sits before and after the human relational event — translating, freeing time, detecting gaps, preparing, documenting — but never constitutes the event itself.
subtitle: AI arrows stop at the boundary; only human presence crosses

figureTitle: Two halves, two owners, two learners
figureType: structural schematic (split panel with two learner rows)
desc: Instructional clarity splits into an AI-delegable planned half and a human-only live half, and the same delegation builds the live half for a novice through struggle but is mere time-saving for an expert.
subtitle: Planned half (delegable) vs. live half (human-only)

rows.left: Novice: planned-half work builds the live half
Expert: planned half is detached time-cost
rows.right: Live half already built; pure time arbitrage

figureTitle: The live-half feedback loop
figureType: cycle (real-time feedback loop with emphasized detection node)
desc: Expert clarity is a real-time loop — explain, check, detect, adjust, re-explain — whose detection step is the heart and which a script-bound teacher structurally cannot run.
subtitle: Detect is the heart; a script reaches explain but is blocked from detect

figureTitle: Draft-then-correct workflow and the delta log
figureType: flowchart (two-path comparison with branch output)
desc: Expert free recall yields an incomplete curriculum, but an AI draft corrected by the expert converts extraction into editing — and the corrections themselves (the delta log) are the curriculum's most valuable column.
subtitle: Path A free recall vs. Path B AI draft then expert correction

stages.label: Path B: AI draft then expert correction
Validated curriculum + delta log (most valuable output)

data.note: Corrections surface tacit knowledge only against a concrete wrong thing

figureTitle: Four rows, one repaired model
figureType: conceptual map (central node with four repair spokes)
desc: All four influences repair part of one double-blindness — an accurate model of the task and of the learner's current knowledge — with each row fixing a different component.
subtitle: Four repair spokes into one shared model

nodes.label: Accurate model of task and learner

figureTitle: The Bastani curve in a help channel
figureType: statistical (two-line divergence chart)
desc: An executive-help AI channel lifts the supervised metric (practice performance) while the unaided metric (proctored certification) flattens then drops — the same assisted/unassisted divergence in domain-specific form.
subtitle: Practice performance vs. unaided certification over weeks

data.axisLabel: Score over weeks of AI-channel use

figureTitle: Bastani divergence: assisted vs. unassisted
figureType: Two-line divergence chart (time series), y-axis from zero.
desc: When the answer button is available, in-tool practice scores climb (+48%) while the external unassisted-assessment line diverges downward (−17%). The gap is invisible in platform metrics.
subtitle: Attempt-first lines stay together; answer-available lines split

data.axisLabel: Vocabulary performance over weeks of use

figureTitle: The vocabulary-encounter pipeline
figureType: Horizontal left-to-right flowchart with a two-band split per stage.
desc: A single word moves from first exposure through spaced retrieval-plus-feedback cycles to unprompted use weeks later. AI generates, schedules and checks; the learner's retrieval and meaning-making stay irreducible.

data.note: AI generates, schedules, checks; learner performs the retrieval and meaning-making

figureTitle: Receptive half, productive half, silent deletion
figureType: Structural before/after comparison panel (two states of one encounter).
desc: An encounter has a receptive half (meet the word) and a load-bearing productive half (retrieve and use it). Substitution deletes only the productive half while usage and quiz metrics stay intact.
subtitle: Metrics row is identical though the productive half vanished

figureTitle: Four group influences with effect sizes
figureType: Horizontal dot/bar plot against the 0.40 hinge reference line, y-axis from zero.
desc: The group's four influences span d = 0.44 to 0.62, all above the 0.40 hinge. Two broad technology rows carry a pre-generative provenance flag.

figureTitle: The four-link implementation chain
figureType: Horizontal left-to-right flowchart with a two-row human/AI split beneath each link.
desc: Program effects come from a four-link chain: Teach, Check, Diagnose, Respond. Each link can be human or automated, and Diagnose and Respond are the load-bearing mechanism that automating replaces rather than augments.

data.note: Diagnose and Respond are the load-bearing links; per link a human-performs and an AI-performs cell

figureTitle: Safe AI role vs. substitution trap, by link
figureType: Four-row structured matrix / comparison schematic.
desc: For each chain link there is a legitimate AI support role and a corresponding substitution trap where AI performs the act instead of supporting it, each with a distinct failure signature.
subtitle: Diagnose and Respond rows carry the heaviest stakes

rows.right: AI defines what counts as known
AI rules the cause unread by humans

figureTitle: Four program-level influences with effect sizes
figureType: Horizontal dot/bar plot against the 0.40 hinge line, value axis from zero.
desc: The group's four influences span d = 0.43 to 0.59, all above the 0.40 hinge, with mathematics programs as the dominant anchor.

figureTitle: Consequential interdependence: two-sided exchange
figureType: Two-node bidirectional systems diagram with a central concept node and an external support ring.
desc: Peer tutoring runs a two-directional exchange: the tutor performs retrieval, reorganization, example-generation and monitoring; the tutee brings near-peer language under social cost and obligation. AI supports only from outside.

figureTitle: Budget case: what the chatbot buys vs. center
figureType: Structural comparison panel (cost columns plus an invisible-assets column).
desc: The spreadsheet prices only explanation-delivery ($11/student), but the peer center also produces invisible assets — tutor learning, an obligation network, help-seeking formation, belonging — that no line item captures.

rows.left: No line item for the rest

figureTitle: Human tutoring vs. ITS vs. AI-replaced peer
figureType: Horizontal dot plot against the 0.40 hinge line, value axis from zero.
desc: VanLehn found human tutoring (0.79) and step-based ITS (0.76) statistically comparable on the tutee side, yet peer tutoring is hypothesized to drop to ~0.10 when the peer exchange is removed.

figureTitle: Internalization arc: fading vs. permanent scaffold
figureType: Two-path trajectory chart (time on x-axis, locus-of-control on y-axis).
desc: External regulation becomes self-regulation only through a managed handoff. A fading scaffold curves from external toward internalized control; a permanent scaffold stays flat at external, and the week-sixteen gap is what the effect sizes describe.

data.axisLabel: Who runs the regulatory loop, over time

rows.right: Still externally regulated; gap is the loss

figureTitle: Six self-regulation influences with effect sizes
figureType: Horizontal dot/bar plot against the 0.40 hinge line, value axis from zero.
desc: The group's six learner-side process influences span d = 0.45 to 0.57, all above the 0.40 hinge. Writing programs is flagged: AI ghostwriting is the antithesis of the mechanism.

figureTitle: Dependence trajectory: engagement vs. independence
figureType: Multi-curve time-series chart, value axis from zero.
desc: In coach-mode, generative-request frequency falls as internalization develops; in the unconfigured default it stays flat or rises. The business metric (engagement) and the learning metric (independence) move in opposite directions.

data.axisLabel: Generative-request frequency over weeks of use

figureTitle: One mechanism, two parallel loops
figureType: Two parallel cyclical diagrams with a connecting bridge.
desc: The same diagnose-act-observe-adjust loop runs on students (Screen, Diagnose, Intervene, Measure, Adjust) and on teachers (Teach, Record, Watch, Diagnose, Re-teach), sharing one structure and one failure mode.
subtitle: Same mechanism, same vulnerability in both rings

figureTitle: Three substitution traps that keep the paperwork
figureType: Three-row structured matrix.
desc: Each of three traps keeps the loop's paperwork intact while removing the cognition, leaving a distinct failure signature in the data.

figureTitle: Three high-band influences with effect sizes
figureType: Horizontal dot/bar plot against the 0.40 hinge line, value axis from zero.
desc: The group's three influences span d = 0.77 to 1.29, far above the 0.40 hinge, with response to intervention the standout.

figureTitle: Jigsaw structure and the courier bypass
figureType: Two-stage structural diagram with a labeled bypass path.
desc: Jigsaw manufactures necessity by deleting redundancy — each expert is the only source for their segment. The AI-prepared-document bypass breaks the hull: fluent content is delivered while expertise never forms.

nodes.label: Home group (each depends on one source)

figureTitle: Revised unit: AI wraps around human exchange
figureType: Horizontal left-to-right three-stage flowchart with an AI-absent center stage.
desc: In the rebuilt unit, AI builds the expert packet and provides rehearsal and a mastery gate, but the home-group exchange at the center is AI-absent. AI returns only afterward to document for reflection.

stages.label: AI builds expert packet; candidate studies
Rehearse with AI; pass closed-notes mastery gate

data.note: AI present before and after; absent in the center stage

figureTitle: Jigsaw, credibility, and AI-replacement hypotheses
figureType: Horizontal dot plot spanning a zero crossing, against the 0.40 hinge.
desc: Jigsaw (1.20) and teacher credibility (0.90) sit near the top, while AI-replacement hypotheses drop sharply — jigsaw to ~0.30 and credibility to ~−0.15, the only negative estimate in this part of the book.

figureTitle: Effort bargain: AI repairs two, third stays human
figureType: Three-vertex conceptual diagram (triangle) with damage and repair paths.
desc: Persistence rests on three terms — expectancy (doable), value (worth doing), and cost of quitting (someone would notice). AI can repair expectancy and value, but the third vertex requires a specific person noticing.

figureTitle: Marcus workflow: detection feeds, human responds
figureType: Horizontal left-to-right timeline flowchart with a platform/human lane split.
desc: Neither party could have produced the outcome alone — the platform detects a latency anomaly and triages it with evidence, then a named advisor holds the conversation, diagnoses the real cause, and rebuilds the schedule.

data.note: Handoff from platform lane to human lane partway across

figureTitle: Two high-band influences with effect sizes
figureType: Horizontal dot/bar plot against the 0.40 hinge line, value axis from zero.
desc: The group's two influences — effort (0.77) and comprehensive instructional programs for teachers (0.72) — sit high above the 0.40 hinge, the same mechanism at two altitudes.

figureTitle: The three-part memory engineering model
figureType: Three-band horizontal schematic with a per-band AI/learner split.
desc: Memory for arbitrary material has three parts — encoding structure (AI-manufactured, received), retrieval effort (learner-only), and spacing schedule (AI-automated). Because the machine's and learner's work differ, there is no cognitive-work conflict.

layers.label: Retrieval effort
layers.note: Learner-only; the irreducible core, cannot be delegated

layers.label: Spacing schedule
layers.note: AI automates the timing; learner follows

figureTitle: Three substitution traps, all auditable
figureType: Three-row structured matrix.
desc: The low-risk classification still leaves three traps — recognition creep, fluency mistaken for the course, unreviewed scaffolds — each with an observable signature and an auditable fix, none a structural limit of the technology.
subtitle: Every fix column is filled: these are choices, not limits

rows.right: Policy to review and retire scaffolds

figureTitle: Two high-band memory influences with effect sizes
figureType: Horizontal dot/bar plot against the 0.40 hinge line, value axis from zero.
desc: Mnemonics (0.76) and rehearsal and memorization (0.73) sit high above the 0.40 hinge, with a small hypothesized AI-delivery discount for mnemonics (~0.70).

figureTitle: Expectancy feedback loop: open vs. closed
figureType: Cyclical diagram with two labeled versions (open and closed).
desc: A teacher prediction allocates rigor and wait time, the student reads it and adjusts effort, the outcome confirms or challenges the prediction. A label converts the open loop (updated by evidence) into a closed one (reinforced regardless).
subtitle: Same loop runs two ways; a label is the switch

stages.label: Task rigor and wait time allocated

figureTitle: Same tool, two configurations: default vs. adopted
figureType: Two-branch comparison / decision-tree schematic.
desc: The same early-warning platform produces opposite effects by configuration. The vendor default labels at scale; the adopted settings inform without pre-forming.

figureTitle: Two above-hinge influences with effect sizes
figureType: Horizontal dot/bar plot against the 0.40 hinge line, value axis from zero.
desc: Deep motivation and approach (0.69) and teachers not labeling students (0.61) both sit above the 0.40 hinge.

figureTitle: In-app mastery vs. paper: converge then diverge
figureType: Two-line time-series chart with a labeled divergence inset, value axis from zero.
desc: Across twenty weeks, in-app mastery and biweekly paper-dictation accuracy converge after targeted small-group instruction, but a mid-year update flips the app to recognition mode and the lines diverge — in-app climbing while paper goes flat.
subtitle: Convergence validates the tool; late divergence is recognition creep

figureTitle: Four-clause practice engine: AI administers, learner performs
figureType: Four-stage practice-loop diagram with a per-clause AI/learner split.
desc: Skill practice works when it is retrieval-based, immediately corrected, distributed, and at the learner's edge. At every clause the learner's action is irreducible while AI only administers.

data.note: AI generates items, schedules returns, tracks mastery, adjusts difficulty; learner action is irreducible

figureTitle: Two above-hinge skill-practice influences
figureType: Horizontal dot/bar plot against the 0.40 hinge line, value axis from zero.
desc: Spelling programs (0.58) and interactive video methods (0.54) sit above the 0.40 hinge, each with a modest hypothesized AI-mediated discount (~0.50 and ~0.45).

figureTitle: The self-sealing prediction loop vs. the provisional alternative
figureType: cycle diagram with adjacent linear chain (systems/causal diagram)
desc: Routing tasks by predicted proficiency creates a circular, self-confirming loop, whereas a high-expectation teacher's provisional belief runs a linear, evidence-updating chain that never seals.
subtitle: Self-confirming loop versus open evidence-updating chain

figureTitle: Two parallel calibration problems
figureType: two-column comparison panel with shared base row (conceptual map)
desc: Problem-solving teaching and teacher expectations are the same structural problem — calibration — each with a mechanism, an AI default that opposes it, and a resulting conflict.
subtitle: Calibration = withholding certainty while evidence arrives

data.axisLabel: Calibration = withholding certainty while evidence arrives

figureTitle: Two influences against the Hattie hinge
figureType: horizontal bar chart with reference line
desc: The group's two influences — problem-solving teaching (d = 0.68) and teacher expectations (d = 0.43) — both sit above the d = 0.40 hinge, but with a meaningful gap between them.
subtitle: Both clear the hinge, with a gap between them

figureTitle: The configuration fork: guardrailed vs. unguardrailed
figureType: four-curve line chart (two-panel divergence/convergence comparison)
desc: Same model, students, and material split by one configuration setting: guardrailed practice and exam curves converge (+127% practice, ~0 exam) while unguardrailed diverge (+48% practice, -17% exam).
subtitle: Practice rising, exam falling — the Bastani signature

figureTitle: The pilot loop: diagnosis, teaching, verification
figureType: three-panel horizontal flow with return loop (systems diagram)
desc: The chemistry pilot's value came from a three-stage loop — platform diagnoses, humans teach, platform verifies — feeding human decisions rather than replacing them.
subtitle: The platform feeds human decisions, not replaces them

figureTitle: Tutoring effect sizes vs. hinge and human-tutor benchmark
figureType: horizontal dot/bar plot with two reference lines
desc: ITS systems average d = 0.48 but reach d = 0.76 under step-level conditions — near the human-tutor benchmark of d = 0.79 — while the group's three rows all clear the 0.40 hinge.
subtitle: Step-level ITS (0.76) nears the human-tutor mark (0.79)

figureTitle: Mechanism deletion: embodied vs. AI-mediated delivery
figureType: grouped horizontal bar chart with reference line
desc: Through embodied co-presence, cohesion (0.44) and adventure (0.43) clear the hinge as large effects; delivered through AI both collapse to a hypothesized d ~ 0.05 — a near-total deletion, not a discount.
subtitle: Embodied effects collapse to near zero through AI

figureTitle: Cohesion as risk-tolerance infrastructure
figureType: two-state side-by-side comparison panel (structural schematic)
desc: In a low-cohesion classroom, participation is a gamble that exits through a social-cost door; in a high-cohesion classroom the gamble collapses and high-value behaviors become structurally available.
subtitle: Low trust sets the cognitive-output floor

rows.left: Output floor set by lowest safety tolerance

figureTitle: Nora's assembled evidence file
figureType: four-quadrant component layout (conceptual map)
desc: AI assembled four components for the program's defense: a participation-outcome correlation table, an equity-gap map, alumni theme clusters, and an administrative-hour audit converting recovered hours into expanded sessions.
subtitle: Four AI-assembled components build the defense

children.label: Alumni theme clusters (30 years of survey text)
Admin-hour audit: recovered hours to expanded sessions

figureTitle: Bandura's four efficacy sources, by strength
figureType: vertical ranked stack with adjacent capability columns (systems diagram)
desc: Collective efficacy grows from four sources ranked by strength — mastery experience (strongest, self-compounding), vicarious experience, social persuasion (weak alone), affective state — and the dashboard touches only source 3.
subtitle: The dashboard touches only social persuasion

children.label: Social persuasion (weak alone; dashboard touches this)

figureTitle: The largest hypothesized collapse: 1.57 to 0.15
figureType: two-bar comparison chart with hinge reference line
desc: Collective teacher efficacy carries the dataset's largest effect size (d = 1.57) as a converted correlation, but hypothesized survival under AI-dashboard-only delivery is d ~ 0.15 — the largest hypothesized collapse in the book.
subtitle: Measured 1.57 vs. AI-dashboard-only survival 0.15

figureTitle: Belief is the fuel, not the engine
figureType: horizontal causal fan-in (systems diagram)
desc: Collective belief changes outcomes only by running through four behavioral channels — attempt more, persist longer, attribute struggle to instruction, cooperate at higher bandwidth — every one terminating in teacher behavior.
subtitle: Four channels converge on teacher behavior

figureTitle: Teacher-model complementarity over the school year
figureType: multi-line trend chart (prediction-accuracy over time)
desc: Teacher judgment starts high and plateaus while the model starts low and rises steeply; the combined ranking runs above both after the first grading period — teachers win at the information-poor start, models catch up late.
subtitle: Teachers win early; models catch up; combined wins late

data.axisLabel: Prediction accuracy across the school year

markers.label: Year-end
markers.sub: Model highest of the single signals

figureTitle: The two-student audit: where model and teacher disagree
figureType: two-case side-by-side comparison panel (structural schematic)
desc: Two week-nine cases show why model output and teacher knowledge must stay distinct — a quiet high performer flagged red by an absence artifact, and a struggling student shown green by mere completion. The disagreement is the signal.
subtitle: The disagreement is the most valuable signal

rows.left: Teacher: knows the flag is wrong

figureTitle: Two influences against the hinge
figureType: horizontal bar chart with hinge reference line
desc: Teacher estimates of achievement (d = 1.29) and teacher clarity (d = 0.75) both sit in the High band, far above the 0.40 hinge, with estimates near the top of the entire list.
subtitle: Estimates near the top of the whole list

figureTitle: The algorithmic Matthew effect loop and its exit
figureType: cycle diagram with dotted exit branch (systems diagram)
desc: An adaptive platform initialized on prior score serves easier content, generates less growth, and reads that as confirmation — a self-sealing loop that opens only through a structured exit (human override plus scheduled re-estimation).
subtitle: Self-sealing loop with a structured exit

figureTitle: Same data, two uses: diagnosis vs. measurement
figureType: two-column contrast fork from a shared source (conceptual map)
desc: Identical assessment data becomes either a short-shelf-life instructional plan (act, re-diagnose, revise) or a long-shelf-life placement label (route, schedule, confirm), depending entirely on what the institution does with it.
subtitle: Shelf life decides whether data hardens into destiny

data.axisLabel: Same assessment data, two institutional uses

figureTitle: Same number, opposite classification
figureType: horizontal bar chart with classification color encoding and hinge line
desc: Prior ability (d = 0.94, context/destiny-risk) sits one rank above strategy to integrate with prior knowledge (d = 0.93, teachable lever) — near-identical numbers, opposite classifications — while prior achievement (d = 0.55) shows how construct definition moves the number.
subtitle: 0.94 and 0.93: nearly identical, opposite classifications

items.label: Strategy to integrate with prior knowledge (lever)

figureTitle: Three properties a chatbot cannot have
figureType: three-panel structural comparison (comparison panels)
desc: Live seminar is public, plural, and shared-context-building — properties a private student-AI dialogue lacks by construction. The d = 0.82 was earned by the live version only.
subtitle: The d = 0.82 was earned by the live seminar only

data.axisLabel: Evidence base: d = 0.82 belongs to the live form

figureTitle: AI at the edges of the talk, never its center
figureType: weekly timeline with role-placement arrows (cycle/flow diagram)
desc: Across a seminar week AI appears at three points — Monday rehearsal, Thursday analytics, weekend prep — all pointing toward the single live seminar on Wednesday, never replacing it.
subtitle: Every AI stage serves the Wednesday seminar

stages.label: Monday: AI rehearsal (student generates, AI cross-examines)
Thursday: AI analytics (talk map to instructor)

figureTitle: The asymmetry vendors omit
figureType: two-bar comparison with hinge reference line
desc: The Murphy et al. meta-analysis behind the d = 0.82 shows strong, reliable comprehension gains but rare, unreliable critical-thinking gains — an asymmetry the aggregate number hides.
subtitle: Comprehension reliable; critical thinking rare

figureTitle: The two-line graph: assisted vs. unassisted
figureType: two-line trend chart with policy-change inflection (time series)
desc: Assisted (hint-available) accuracy climbs from week one while unassisted (cold-set) accuracy stays flat until the configuration changes at week eight — attempt-first, fading active, cold-set mastery — after which the unassisted line finally rises.
subtitle: The cold-set line is the only one that matters

markers.label: Week 1
markers.sub: Assisted rises; unassisted flat and low

figureTitle: The scaffolding contract: two phases and the failure mode
figureType: horizontal two-phase progression with a divergent failure branch (mechanism diagram)
desc: Legitimate scaffolding runs two phases — supported then faded — while the commercial failure mode keeps phase one running indefinitely and declares mastery from assisted answers ('dependence with good UX').
subtitle: Fading is the load-bearing condition

stages.label: Phase 1: supported (high contingency, help at the edge)
Failure mode: Phase 1 forever, mastery declared from assisted answers

data.note: The failure branch keeps Phase 1 running and never fades.

figureTitle: The Macnamara correction: variance explained
figureType: horizontal bar chart (percentage of variance explained)
desc: Deliberate practice explains about 26% of performance variance in games, 21% in music, 18% in sports — and only about 4% in education — capping the practice-quantity story exactly where practice platforms are sold.
subtitle: Education's 4% caps the practice-quantity story

figureTitle: Two students, two preparation paths
figureType: two-path comparison panel (process comparison)
desc: Student A reads an AI summary five times (recognition only) while Student B writes from memory, checks against the chapter, and fixes gaps — engaging retrieval, generation, monitoring, and error correction. B is more prepared though A feels more prepared.
subtitle: B is more prepared though A feels more prepared

figureTitle: Three acts on the production side
figureType: production-side three-act schematic with deletion contrast (mechanism diagram)
desc: Producing a summary forces three cognitive acts at once — selection, generation, self-testing — all of which a received summary deletes by completing them elsewhere.
subtitle: A received summary deletes all three acts

data.axisLabel: Three cognitive acts: run vs. deleted

figureTitle: Automaticity: working-memory reallocation
figureType: before/after capacity-partition comparison (mechanism cross-section)
desc: Under heavy decoding load a child's working memory is mostly consumed by decoding, leaving little for comprehension; after repeated reading with error feedback automatizes decoding, capacity is released and comprehension expands.
subtitle: Repeated reading releases capacity for comprehension

rows.right: Decoding shrinks to a small region

figureTitle: The substitution ledger: five roles, three verdicts
figureType: five-row classification roster (conceptual map / structural schematic)
desc: Repeated reading breaks into five roles with different substitutability verdicts — reader (never), text (AI-generatable with QC), listener/error-catcher (AI with caveats), prosody model (human-preferred), motivation/relationship (human) — a roster rather than a gate.
subtitle: A roster of roles, not a single gate

figureTitle: The listening economy, before and after
figureType: before/after allocation comparison (proportional schematic)
desc: Before AI, the teacher's scarce listening time is spread thin across all 26 children; after, AI listens to the 18 stronger readers while the teacher concentrates on the 8 lowest — expanding the listening economy rather than replacing its human share.
subtitle: AI expands listening; the teacher concentrates on the 8 lowest

data.axisLabel: Allocation of listening time across 26 children

rows.right: AI listens to the 18 stronger readers

figureTitle: Two phonics numbers and the grade-band moderator
figureType: horizontal dot plot / forest-style comparison against the 0.40 hinge
desc: Phonics carries two defensible effect sizes — Hattie's broad d=0.70 and the NRP's tight d=0.41 — and the NRP timing moderator (d=0.55 in K-1 vs d=0.27 after Grade 1) is the figure the vendor pitch omits.
subtitle: Effect sizes against the 0.40 hinge

figureTitle: Phonics mechanism stack and the AI/teacher split
figureType: vertical layered stack with two parallel role columns
desc: Phonics learning runs as five interdependent layers — phonemic awareness through automaticity — and at each layer AI can support practice and assessment while only a human teacher performs first teaching, error diagnosis, and reteaching.
subtitle: Five layers, two roles

layers.label: Automaticity
layers.note: AI: practice / tracking · Teacher: builds it

layers.label: Blending
layers.note: AI: practice generation · Teacher: error diagnosis

layers.label: Grapheme-phoneme mapping
layers.note: AI: progress tracking · Teacher: first modeling

figureTitle: Minute-audit: two configurations of the literacy block
figureType: before/after stacked-bar comparison panels
desc: Adopting the app as core instruction does not add systematicity — it replaces the explicit-instruction column of the weekly literacy block with app-practice minutes, leaving first-teaching near zero while total minutes stay constant.
subtitle: Same total minutes, first-teaching vanishes

figureTitle: Feedback distribution: centers and the negative tail
figureType: dot plot of meta-analytic centers plus a proportion bar for the negative tail
desc: The most-cited feedback number (d=0.70) runs high against the field's meta-analytic centers (Kluger-DeNisi d=0.41, Wisniewski d=0.48), and over one-third of feedback interventions decrease performance — the distribution the average conceals.
subtitle: Effect sizes against the 0.40 hinge

figureTitle: Same essay, three comments, opposite signs
figureType: three-column comparison panel (annotated example matrix)
desc: Three machine-generatable comments on the same essay, delivered at identical speed, differ completely in Hattie-Timperley level, where they direct attention, predicted sign, and whether they supply a usable next move.
subtitle: Constant speed, diverging sign

figureTitle: Three substitution failure modes and their signatures
figureType: three parallel vertical failure-channel diagram
desc: Automated feedback fails through three parallel channels — unreviewed comments triggering the harm distribution, the feedback channel becoming an answer channel (Bastani +48/-17 signature), and volume mistaken for value — each with its own observable signature.
subtitle: Three channels, three signatures

data.note: Third channel: comment volume multiplies, plausible surface hides quality failures, revision behavior unchanged.

figureTitle: Three-stage ownership chain and the AI bypass
figureType: horizontal left-to-right process chain with a bypass branch
desc: A working goal runs formulation (self-diagnosis) to commitment (Locke-Latham mechanisms activate) to self-monitoring (the goal becomes the comparator) — and an AI-generated goal bypasses formulation, landing at commitment with commitment uncertain.
subtitle: AI-generated goal skips formulation

stages.label: Commitment: goal is mine (attention, effort, persistence, strategy)

data.note: AI-generated goal skips Stage 1 and joins at commitment — commitment uncertain (dotted bypass).

figureTitle: Conference prep: dashboard vs. revision trail
figureType: side-by-side comparison panels
desc: An advisor entering a conference with a green-completion dashboard learns about the platform; an advisor entering with the student's revision trail (pass chemistry to get an 80 to do stoichiometry without copying Marisol) learns about the student.
subtitle: Property of the platform vs. property of the student

figureTitle: Three mapping conditions and relative transfer
figureType: ranked comparison panels (three conditions on a shared relative-outcome scale)
desc: Outcomes split by who does the relational work: closed-book student construction yields highest delayed transfer, open-book is intermediate (Karpicke-Blunt warning), and studying AI-generated or expert maps yields the lowest relational transfer.
subtitle: Relative delayed-relational-transfer outcome

figureTitle: Safe AI roles across the three production operations
figureType: three-operation matrix with an off-to-the-side excluded element
desc: Across the three production operations of mapping (selection, proposition generation, misconception display), AI may evaluate, question, and aggregate but never pre-select, pre-label, or pre-correct — and the generate button sits off to the side.
subtitle: AI may vs. AI does not

rows.right: Generate button: detached, off to the side (studying condition)

figureTitle: The four-move classroom sequence
figureType: horizontal four-step process flow
desc: Priya's redesigned unit runs four ordered moves — closed-book construction, open-book revision in a second color, AI evaluation with one targeted question, Friday critique of a planted-error generated map — with the generate button appearing only at the final move.
subtitle: Generate button enters only at move 4

stages.label: Friday critique of planted-error AI map

data.note: Generate button enters only at move 4, as sparring partner not ghostwriter.

figureTitle: Three channels of parental involvement and AI's reach
figureType: three parallel vertical channels with access/reach annotation
desc: High-value parental involvement runs through three channels — expectations (inside the family, no school access), home learning behavior (school equips, family performs), and home-school trust (the school owns it) — and AI threatens the third if counterfeited.
subtitle: Which channels AI touches

nodes.label: Expectations / aspirations (inside family, no school access)
Home learning behavior (school equips, family performs)

figureTitle: Five features, five boundaries: safe use vs. substitution
figureType: five-row feature matrix with a shared boundary axis
desc: Each platform feature (translation, drafting, conversation prompts, logistics, chatbot) has a clear boundary, and the boundary is always whether a human's actual knowledge of the child stands behind the message.
subtitle: Does a human's knowledge of the child stand behind it?

data.axisLabel: Boundary: human's knowledge of the child

rows.right: Chatbot answers 'how is my child doing'

figureTitle: Two paths: encoding present vs. encoding absent
figureType: two-path parallel-process diagram converging on a shared artifact
desc: When the student takes notes, three operations run live (selection, paraphrase, stall signal at confusion); when AI takes notes, the transcript fills automatically and no confusion signal fires — both paths converge on the same artifact, only one produced encoding.
subtitle: Same artifact, divergent process

data.axisLabel: Both converge on the same finished-notes artifact

figureTitle: The gated lecture-day timeline
figureType: horizontal timeline with phased function bands
desc: The pilot inverts the delivery order across a lecture day — Hour 0 the student takes own notes (encoding), Hours 1-4 the transcript is locked behind a retrieval gate, Hour 4+ the transcript unlocks for diff-and-repair (storage).
subtitle: Encoding to retrieval to storage

figureTitle: Selection quality over ten weeks
figureType: two-line time series
desc: Under constraint plus AI critique, the proportion of student marks landing on structural claims rises across ten weeks; in sections where Smart Highlights pre-marks are visible, selection quality stays flat or declines.
subtitle: Constrained + critique vs. pre-marked (illustrative)

data.axisLabel: Proportion of marks on claims, weeks 1-10

markers.label: Week 10
markers.sub: Constraint + critique high; pre-marked flat or declining

figureTitle: Two paths: discrimination retained vs. fluency illusion
figureType: two-path mechanism diagram with divergent terminal states
desc: Constrained marking forces a judgment of importance and is reviewed by closed-book retrieval (illusion broken); free or pre-marked text is reread, each pass feels smoother, confidence rises, and the gap to actual retrieval is the illusion.
subtitle: Same surface behavior, divergent endpoints

rows.right: Retrieval untested — the gap is the illusion

figureTitle: Three marking conditions and the mechanism profile
figureType: three-row condition matrix
desc: Three conditions diverge on whether the discrimination mechanism runs and whether the fluency illusion is installed: constrained marking (mechanism runs, benefit), free marking (no discrimination, low utility), and AI pre-marked text (discrimination removed, illusion pre-installed).
subtitle: Discrimination running? Illusion installed?

figureTitle: Where the variance lives: between vs. within schools
figureType: single proportional bar (between vs. within)
desc: Only on the order of ten to twenty percent of achievement variance lies between schools; the large majority lies within them — the empirical basis for treating school effects as a context to read rather than a lever to pull.
subtitle: Share of achievement variance

figureTitle: The three-strand braid inside the school-effects number
figureType: three-strand braid / layered-decomposition diagram
desc: The between-school signal is a braid of three unequal strands — composition (a confound that dominates raw rankings, maps to housing), concentration (structural, board-level policy), and institutional practice (smallest, the only strand 'quality' honestly describes).
subtitle: Composite index decomposed

layers.label: Composition (thickest)
layers.note: Confound, dominates raw rankings, maps to housing

layers.label: Concentration (medium)
layers.note: Structural, responds only to board-level policy

layers.label: Institutional practice (thinnest)
layers.note: Smallest, hardest to isolate — the only 'quality' strand

figureTitle: The three channels of technology's contribution
figureType: three-column channel model with a bundle input
desc: Technology contributes through three indirect channels — access (binary, utility standard, equity instrument), amplification (the effect belongs to the mechanism, not the device), and displacement (every device-hour replaces something) — and a bundle prevents separate evaluation.
subtitle: Bundle fans into all three

figureTitle: The one-question provenance audit
figureType: three-column checkbox audit schematic
desc: A vendor's d=0.47 citation must pass a three-column provenance test — this product? this category? any LLM-era product? — and for a pre-LLM aggregate applied to a 2026 AI suite, all three columns come up empty (0 of 3).
subtitle: Cited d = 0.47 scores 0 of 3

figureTitle: The two-ledger structure
figureType: two-panel ledger comparison
desc: Infrastructure and pedagogy are kept on two separate ledgers — infrastructure judged on a utility standard (uptime, cost, coverage, accessibility), pedagogy judged on mechanism and learning — and no contract may force the ledgers to merge.
subtitle: No contract may merge them

rows.left: Counts in neither: aggregate d, device counts, login rates
rows.right: Counts in neither: aggregate d, device counts, login rates

figureTitle: The authorship gate as a line
figureType: bisected schematic with directional role arrows
desc: A single line — 'produced by the student' — separates safe AI roles (feedback on existing text, interrogative questions, reading support, mechanics, opposing briefs) from the conflict zone (generating drafts, continuing sentences, rewriting, producing adoptable text).
subtitle: Safe roles point at existing text; conflict roles at text being created

stages.label: Safe roles: feedback, questions, reading support, mechanics, opposing briefs
Conflict roles: generate drafts, continue sentences, rewrite, produce adopted text
Text that does not yet exist

data.note: Every safe-side arrow points at text that already exists; every conflict-side arrow is blocked at the gate.

figureTitle: The detection collapse: 94 percent undetected
figureType: single proportional bar (detected vs. undetected)
desc: Scarfe and colleagues injected fully AI-written submissions into a real university exam system and 94 percent went undetected — retiring detection-based policy as a failed option, not merely a costly one.
subtitle: Scarfe et al. 2024

figureTitle: Two failure modes, one gate between them
figureType: two-timeline comparison with a central gate
desc: The detection faction's failure (false accusation: week 1 accusation, week 4 case collapsed, student harmed) and the surrender faction's failure (hollowing: take-home scores rise while supervised scores slide) are both real, and the authorship gate avoids both.
subtitle: The gate avoids both failures

data.axisLabel: Central node: authorship gate avoids both

rows.left: Gate: witnessed writing, process portfolio, disclosure
rows.right: Gate: witnessed writing, process portfolio, disclosure

figureTitle: AI feasibility of 252 Hattie influences
figureType: Small-multiple horizontal bar chart (four stacked-category panels) — counts out of 252.
desc: Across 252 Hattie influences, AI feasibility skews toward support and amplification rather than native automation: 97 are AI-adjacent and 88 AI-augmented, while only 29 are AI-native and 38 AI-resistant.
subtitle: Most influences fall into support, not AI-native categories

figureTitle: Effect band by AI feasibility
figureType: Grouped horizontal bar chart (effect band on the vertical axis, four feasibility groups per band), counts.
desc: The strongest learning effects cluster in amplification, not automation. In the High band, AI-Augmented influences dominate (24 of 32), while the Below-hinge band is led by AI-Adjacent (51). The top-impact effects concentrate where AI amplifies rather than substitutes.
subtitle: High-impact effects concentrate in AI-Augmented

figureTitle: The amplification-vs-substitution phase gate
figureType: Horizontal two-branch conceptual schematic with a central gate.
desc: A high-impact learning influence meets a phase gate. If AI amplifies the mechanism (feedback, practice, diagnosis, planning, reflection), the effect is preserved. If AI substitutes for the cognitive or social work that produces the effect, the effect collapses.
subtitle: Does AI amplify the mechanism or substitute for it?

stages.label: Amplification: AI supports the mechanism — effect preserved
Substitution: AI performs the work — effect collapses

data.note: The gate divides amplification (effect preserved) from substitution (effect collapses).

figureTitle: Priority review cases by effect size
figureType: Horizontal dot plot (one row per influence), effect size on the x-axis from zero, dot color encoding mechanism-conflict level.
desc: The top priority-review influences span d=0.70 to d=1.57, all far above the 0.40 hinge. These are where high leverage and high mechanism-conflict risk coincide — the cases warranting the closest human review.
subtitle: Top priority cases, ranked by effect size (d)

figureTitle: The Irreducibly Human tier taxonomy
figureType: Vertical hierarchy / stacked-tier schematic with a marked boundary line.
desc: Human cognitive capacity is organized into tiers. AI is superhuman at the bottom (Tier 1 pattern and association) and structurally weak-to-absent at the top (Tiers 4-7), with a phase gate at the Tier 1 / Tier 4 boundary dividing where machines win from where humans are irreplaceable.
subtitle: Where machines win, where humans are irreplaceable

figureTitle: Friction findings: practice gain vs. exam collapse
figureType: Two-panel proportional bar chart — Panel A paired before/after-style bars (Bastani), Panel B a single magnitude bar (Kosmyna).
desc: Free AI use during practice produced a 48% apparent gain but a 17-point loss on the unassisted exam, and AI-assisted writing cut functional brain connectivity by up to 55%. Output fluency diverged sharply from the neural events of learning.
subtitle: Apparent benefit vs. real cost of AI in practice

figureTitle: The phase gate: Tier 1 delegation vs. Tier 4-7 protection
figureType: Horizontal two-zone systems diagram with a central gate (left-to-right flow).
desc: The division of labor: AI handles Tier 1 pattern work (structure, retrieval, feedback, error flagging), while the human keeps Tier 4-7 work (plausibility auditing, problem formulation, causal identification, accountability). The gate sits at the Tier 1 / Tier 4 boundary; AI is blocked from crossing it.
subtitle: AI handles Tier 1; humans hold Tier 4-7

figureTitle: The AI+1 labor-market signal
figureType: Two-panel proportional chart — Panel A wage-premium year-over-year bars, Panel B diverging job-growth bars.
desc: AI-skilled workers command a 56% wage premium, up from 25% the prior year. Meanwhile AI-skill job postings grew 7.5% while total postings fell 11.3% — the market pays a sharp premium for AI-fluent domain experts.
subtitle: 56% wage premium and the diverging job trend

figureTitle: The wrong arrow vs. the right arrow
figureType: Two-panel before/after comparison schematic (two contrasting horizontal arrow paths).
desc: The default frame routes a domain expert OUT of their domain to become a generic technologist (the wrong arrow, unpaid by the market). The AI+1 model keeps the domain identity and layers AI capability on top (the right arrow) — which is what the 56% wage premium actually pays for.
subtitle: Career model: leave the domain vs. augment it

rows.left: Market does not pay this path
